region_of_origin: India (North and West India — Uttar Pradesh / Maharashtra)
food_subgroup: Deep-fried semolina puffs with spiced tamarind water filling

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no dedicated concept for Pani Puri. The nearest parent is a deep-fried grain snack with filling. A new concept for Indian hollow puffed street food preparations (semolina puri with spiced tamarind water) is recommended for FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_description: Deep-fried grain puff snack with filling, Indian street food (closest parent match)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code exists for Pani Puri. A03RJ (deep-fried grain puff snack) is the closest defensible parent. Exact code should be requested from EFSA.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Pani Puri as a specific food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Pani Puri is 6–8 hollow crispy puris (approximately 150g total including the spiced tamarind water filling), typically consumed as a snack at a chaat counter or street stall.

nutrient_source.title: Pani Puri — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, Entry EI000051
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods database entry EI000051 for Pani Puri. ICMR-NIN is the primary approved source for Indian dishes under Project Veritas source policy.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Deep-Fried Semolina and Potato-Based Indian Street Food — analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Pani Puri in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 62 (medium) is estimated from the closest analogous foods: deep-fried semolina puri (GI ~65–70 range for refined semolina products) and boiled potato filling (GI ~60–65). The composite GI of ~62 reflects the moderating effect of the high water content of the spiced tamarind pani and the chickpea/potato filling. Glycemic load per 150g serving = (62 × 31.2 × 1.5) / 100 = approximately 29.0g. Original input GL of 19.3 was per 100g; this record reports GL per standard 150g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 485mg per 100g; black salt (kala namak) and spiced tamarind water (pani) are primary sodium contributors
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should strictly limit Pani Puri consumption; a standard 150g serving delivers approximately 728mg sodium (nearly 50% of the 1500mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive patients), and street vendor preparations typically contain even higher sodium levels due to liberal use of black salt and tamarind concentrate.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI of 62 and glycemic load of approximately 29 per 150g serving from refined semolina puri and potato filling
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Pani Puri in moderation; the medium GI (62) and moderate glycemic load from the refined semolina puri shells and starchy potato filling require portion control, and substituting sprouted moong for potato filling and limiting the number of puris per serving can help reduce postprandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (485mg/100g), moderate fat from deep-frying (6.5g/100g), and low saturated fat (0.8g/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, Pani Puri poses a moderate concern primarily due to its high sodium content (485mg/100g) which can elevate blood pressure; the fat content from deep-frying is moderate and saturated fat is low (0.8g/100g), but frequent consumption should be avoided given the cumulative sodium load from the spiced tamarind water.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory herbs (mint, coriander) and tamarind in the pani; sulfur compounds from black salt
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Pani Puri is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; the mint, coriander, and tamarind in the spiced water provide antioxidant and mild anti-inflammatory compounds, though the high sodium content warrants moderation if the patient is also managing hypertension.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pani Puri (also known as Gol Gappa, Puchka, or Phuchka) is India's most iconic and beloved street food snack, widely popular across North and West India and enjoyed by the Indian diaspora in Singapore and Southeast Asia. The dish consists of small, round, hollow crispy spheres — approximately 4–5cm in diameter — made from fine semolina (sooji) or wheat flour (maida), deep-fried in oil until they puff up into thin-walled, air-filled shells with a satisfying crunch. Each puri is cracked open with a thumb to create a small hole, then stuffed with a spiced filling of mashed boiled potato and boiled chickpeas (or sprouted moong beans), seasoned with chaat masala and tamarind chutney. The filled puri is then immediately flooded with chilled spiced tamarind water (pani) — a tangy, spicy, refreshing liquid made from fresh mint, coriander, ginger, green chillies, roasted cumin, and black salt (kala namak). The entire puri is popped into the mouth in one bite, delivering an explosion of contrasting flavours: crispy, tangy, spicy, sour, and savoury simultaneously. Nutritionally, Pani Puri provides 188 kcal per 100g with 31.2g carbohydrate, 3.8g protein, and 6.5g fat. The sodium content is notably high at 485mg per 100g, primarily from black salt and tamarind concentrate, making it a concern for hypertensive individuals. The GI is medium (~62), with a glycemic load of approximately 29 per standard 150g serving. It is vegetarian, vegan, halal, and dairy-free, but contains gluten from semolina and wheat flour. Pani Puri is a culturally significant chaat preparation, typically consumed as an afternoon or evening snack at street stalls, chaat counters, and Indian restaurants.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition), the primary approved source for Indian dishes under Project Veritas source policy. The entry EI000051 is an exact match for Pani Puri. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework, clearly documented. Confidence is high for macronutrients and secondary nutrients; GI confidence is 0.9 (analogous estimate).
notes: 1. Cholesterol is 0.0mg per 100g — confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN source; Pani Puri is a plant-based preparation with no animal-derived ingredients, so cholesterol is naturally zero. 2. Vitamin B12 is 0.0mcg — naturally zero as this is a fully plant-based (vegan) food with no animal products. 3. Vitamin D is 0.0mcg — naturally zero as this is a plant-based food not fortified with vitamin D. 4. Trans fat is 0.0g — confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN source for this preparation. 5. GI of 62 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI entry for Pani Puri exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 150g serving is approximately 29.0g (recalculated from per-100g carbohydrate of 31.2g × GI 62 × 1.5 / 100). 6. Portions in the input contained three duplicate entries for '1 serving (150g)'; deduplicated to two distinct portion sizes: 150g (standard serving) and 100g (6 pieces). 7. Copper (0.05mg), manganese (0.1mg), and vitamin K (1.5mcg) are sourced from enriched_json with confidence 0.9; these are not directly reported in the ICMR-NIN entry but are plausible for this food composition. 8. FoodEx2 code A03RJ is a closest-parent match; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for Pani Puri.
